title: Electric piano

infobox.value: is a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") that has a [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano")-style [musical keyboard](/wiki/Musical_keyboard "Musical keyboard"), where sound is produced by means of mechanical hammers striking metal strings or reeds or wire tines, which leads to vibrations which are then converted into electrical signals by [pickups](/wiki/Pick_up_(music_technology) "Pick up (music technology)") (either magnetic, electrostatic, or piezoelectric). The pickups are connected to an [instrument amplifier](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier") and [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") to reinforce the sound sufficiently for the performer and audience to hear.

[Fritz W. Winckel](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritz_Winckel "de:Fritz Winckel") [in German] (1931). "Das Radio-Klavier von Bechstein-Siemens-Nernst". *Die Umschau*. **35**: 840–843. [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [0722-8562](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/0722-8562).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Electro-mechanical keyboard musical instrument

Not to be confused with [electronic piano](/wiki/Electronic_piano "Electronic piano") or [digital piano](/wiki/Digital_piano "Digital piano").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Wurlitzer_model_112_electric_piano_1956.JPG/250px-Wurlitzer_model_112_electric_piano_1956.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wurlitzer_model_112_electric_piano_1956.JPG)

A Wurlitzer model 112 electric piano with a [guitar amplifier](/wiki/Guitar_amplifier "Guitar amplifier")

An **electric piano** is a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") that has a [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano")-style [musical keyboard](/wiki/Musical_keyboard "Musical keyboard"), where sound is produced by means of mechanical hammers striking metal strings or reeds or wire tines, which leads to vibrations which are then converted into electrical signals by [pickups](/wiki/Pick_up_(music_technology) "Pick up (music technology)") (either magnetic, electrostatic, or piezoelectric). The pickups are connected to an [instrument amplifier](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier") and [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") to reinforce the sound sufficiently for the performer and audience to hear.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Phillip_Faber_p%C3%A5_elektrisk_klaver.jpg/250px-Phillip_Faber_p%C3%A5_elektrisk_klaver.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phillip_Faber_p%C3%A5_elektrisk_klaver.jpg)

Phillip Faber playing an electric piano in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), 2025

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/NeoBechstein_Fl%C3%BCgel.jpg/250px-NeoBechstein_Fl%C3%BCgel.jpg)](/wiki/File:NeoBechstein_Fl%C3%BCgel.jpg)

[Neo-Bechstein](/wiki/Neo-Bechstein "Neo-Bechstein") (1931)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Vierlang-Forster_electric_piano_%281937%29.jpg/250px-Vierlang-Forster_electric_piano_%281937%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vierlang-Forster_electric_piano_(1937).jpg)

Vierling-[Förster](/wiki/August_F%C3%B6rster "August Förster") piano (1937)[[3]](#cite_note-braun04-3)[[4]](#cite_note-voigt88-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Storytone_electric_piano_%281939%29_by_Story_%26_Clark_and_RCA%2C_art_deco_design_by_John_Vassos%2C_MIM_PHX.jpg/250px-Storytone_electric_piano_%281939%29_by_Story_%26_Clark_and_RCA%2C_art_deco_design_by_John_Vassos%2C_MIM_PHX.jpg)](/wiki/File:Storytone_electric_piano_(1939)_by_Story_%26_Clark_and_RCA,_art_deco_design_by_John_Vassos,_MIM_PHX.jpg)

Storytone (1939) by Story & Clark and [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation")[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-storytone-7)

The [Neo-Bechstein](/wiki/Neo-Bechstein "Neo-Bechstein") electric piano was built in 1931.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson201942–43-8) The Vierlang-Forster electric piano was introduced in 1937. The RCA Storytone electric piano was built in 1939 in a joint venture between Story & Clark and RCA. The case was designed by [John Vassos](/wiki/John_Vassos "John Vassos"), the American industrial designer. It debuted at the 1939 World's Fair.[[7]](#cite_note-storytone-7)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Yamaha_CP-70M_ready_to_play.jpg/250px-Yamaha_CP-70M_ready_to_play.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yamaha_CP-70M_ready_to_play.jpg)

[Yamaha CP-70M](/wiki/Yamaha_CP-70 "Yamaha CP-70")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Yamaha_CP-70_opened_top.jpg/250px-Yamaha_CP-70_opened_top.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yamaha_CP-70_opened_top.jpg)

Strings and hammers of Yamaha CP-70

The term "electric piano" can refer to several different instruments which vary in their sound-producing mechanisms and consequent timbral characters.

### Struck strings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Struck strings")]

Main article: [Electric grand piano](/wiki/Electric_grand_piano "Electric grand piano")

[Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation"), [Baldwin](/wiki/Baldwin_Piano_Company "Baldwin Piano Company"), [Helpinstill](/wiki/Helpinstill "Helpinstill") and [Kawai](/wiki/Kawai_Musical_Instruments "Kawai Musical Instruments")'s electric pianos are actual grand or upright pianos with strings and hammers. The Helpinstill models have a traditional soundboard; the others have none, and are more akin to a solid-body [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar").

On Yamaha's pianos, such as the [CP-70](/wiki/Yamaha_CP-70 "Yamaha CP-70"), the vibration of the strings is converted to an [electrical signal](/wiki/Signal_(electrical_engineering) "Signal (electrical engineering)") by [piezoelectric](/wiki/Piezoelectric "Piezoelectric") pickups under the bridge.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019334-9) Helpinstill's instruments use a set of [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") pickups attached to the instrument's frame. All these instruments have a tonal character similar to that of an acoustic piano.

### Struck reeds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Struck reeds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Wurlitzer210.png/250px-Wurlitzer210.png)](/wiki/File:Wurlitzer210.png)

Wurlitzer EP-210

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Eboardmuseum_5_cropped.jpg/250px-Eboardmuseum_5_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eboardmuseum_5_cropped.jpg)

Struck reeds of a Wurlitzer electric piano (shown here with the hard cover removed)

[Wurlitzer electronic pianos](/wiki/Wurlitzer_electronic_piano "Wurlitzer electronic piano") (sometimes called "Wurli" as a nickname)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson201997-10) use flat steel reeds struck by felt hammers. The reeds fit within a comb-like metal plate, and the reeds and plate together form an electrostatic or capacitive [pickup](/wiki/Pickup_(music_technology) "Pickup (music technology)") system.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019101-11) The reeds are tuned by adding or removing mass from a lump of solder at the free end of the reed. Replacement reeds are furnished with a slight excess of solder, and thus tuned "flat"; the user is required – by repeated trial and error – to gradually file off the excess solder until the correct tuning is achieved.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019118-12)

In 2015, Brazilian inventor Tiago Valente created the first prototype of the Valente Electric Piano, an electromechanical instrument where the hammers strike reeds, similar to the ones used in a Wurlitzer.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2020, the Valente Electric Piano was launched commercially; at the time of launch, Valente said that he took inspiration from the Suette Piano, another reed electric piano that was made in Brazil in the 1980s.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Struck tuning-forks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Struck tuning-forks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Rhodes_Mk_II_73_cropped.jpg/250px-Rhodes_Mk_II_73_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodes_Mk_II_73_cropped.jpg)

Rhodes Mark II Stage 73

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Fender_Rhodes_%28Inside%29.jpg/250px-Fender_Rhodes_%28Inside%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fender_Rhodes_(Inside).jpg)

[Tuning forks](/wiki/Tuning_fork "Tuning fork") of Fender Rhodes Mark I

The [tuning fork](/wiki/Tuning_fork "Tuning fork") here refers to the struck element having two vibrating parts. In [Fender Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes_piano "Rhodes piano") instruments, the struck portion of the "fork" is a [tine](/wiki/Tine_(structural) "Tine (structural)") of stiff steel wire. The other part of the fork, parallel and adjacent to the tine, is the tonebar, a sturdy steel bar which acts as a resonator and adds sustain to the sound. The tine is fitted with a spring which can be moved along its length to allow the pitch to be varied for [fine-tuning](/wiki/Piano_tuning "Piano tuning").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019213-15)

### Plucked reeds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Plucked reeds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Multimoog_%26_Hohner_Pianet.jpg/250px-Multimoog_%26_Hohner_Pianet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Multimoog_%26_Hohner_Pianet.jpg)

Hohner Pianet (below)

The [Hohner Pianet](/wiki/Hohner_Pianet "Hohner Pianet") uses adhesive pads made from an undressed leather surface cushioned by a [foam rubber](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane") backing.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019123-16) Hohner's later "Pianet T" uses [silicone rubber](/wiki/Silicone_rubber "Silicone rubber") suction pads rather than adhesive pads and replaces the electrostatic system with passive electromagnetic pickups similar to those of the Rhodes.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019140-17)

### Other electric keyboard instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other electric keyboard instruments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Clavinet_d6.jpg/250px-Clavinet_d6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clavinet_d6.jpg)

Hohner Clavinet D6

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Clavinet-Funktionsprinzip.svg/250px-Clavinet-Funktionsprinzip.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Clavinet-Funktionsprinzip.svg)

Tangent action of a Clavinet :  
1. Tuning / 2. Damper / 3. Tangent / 4. Anvil / 5. Key / 6. String / 7. Pickup / 8. Tailpiece

Hohner's "[Clavinet](/wiki/Clavinet "Clavinet")" is essentially an electric [clavichord](/wiki/Clavichord "Clavichord"). A rubber pad under each key presses the string onto a metal anvil, causing the "fretted" portion of the string to vibrate. This is detected by a series of pickups, which convert them into an electrical signal.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenhoffRobertson2019244,_246–247-18)

## Digital pianos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electric_piano&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Digital pianos")]

[Digital pianos](/wiki/Digital_piano "Digital piano") that provide an emulated electric piano sound have largely supplanted the actual electro-mechanical instruments in the 2010s, due to the small size, light weight, and versatility of digital instruments, which can produce a huge range of tones besides piano tones (e.g., emulations of [Hammond organ](/wiki/Hammond_organ "Hammond organ") sounds, [synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") sounds, etc.). However, some performers still perform and record with vintage electric pianos. In 2009, [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes_piano "Rhodes piano") produced a new line of electro-mechanical pianos, known as the Rhodes Mark 7, followed by an offering from [Vintage Vibe](/wiki/Vintage_Vibe "Vintage Vibe").[[19]](#cite_note-19)
